description_en: Kiyokazu (毅一) pairs 'unyielding/manly' (Kiyo) with 'one'. A highly resolute, stoic name aiming to forge a boy of absolute courage and singular determination.
Kiyoshi (喜義) blends 'joy' with 'honor/justice' (Yoshi, often read as Shi). Extremely serious and structurally powerful, promising an incredibly ethical yet happy life.
Kiyoshi (喜吉) couples two deeply auspicious characters: 'joy' and 'good luck'. The ultimate well-wishing classic name, practically overflowing with positive history.
Kiyoshi (喜好) uses 'joy' and 'fond/pleasing'. Suggests an exceedingly warm-hearted, universally beloved character who brings happiness to any environment.
Kiyotaka (喜代孝) heavily anchors the phonetic 'joyful generation' prefix with 'filial piety'. Profoundly traditional, asking the child to deeply respect his ancestors.
Kiyotaka (喜代隆) pairs 'joyful generation' with 'noble/prosperous'. Cultivates an image of immense, multi-generational wealth and uninterrupted upward mobility.
Kiyotaka (貴孝) merges 'noble/precious' with 'filial piety'. Supremely elegant and strictly moral, describing an aristocratic youth profoundly dedicated to his family.
Kiyotsugu (喜代継) uses 'inherit' wrapped with 'joyful generation'. Unmistakably clear in its intent to pass down a deeply joyful, culturally rich family legacy.

meaning_en: The second son of joyous generations.
description_en: Kiyotsugu (喜代次) pairs the celebratory 'joyful generation' with 'next/second'. A profoundly stable, highly classical choice for an adored second-born son.
Kiyotsugu (起容嗣) uses extraordinarily unique phonetics: 'rouse-form-heir'. Exudes an incredibly intricate, highly aristocratic, and historically profound aura.
Kiyoto (瑛士) couples 'crystal' with 'samurai/gentleman'. Wonderfully sharp and beautifully clear; a very modern styling of a culturally classic, honorable gentleman.
Kiyoto (喜代人) applies the 'joyful generation' stem to 'person'. Highlights an individual inherently tied to the happiness of their family's long history.
Kiyoto (喜代登) replaces 'person' with 'ascend/climb'. Imparts exceptional momentum, depicting someone gracefully scaling the heights of life while remaining happy.
Kiyonari (淳也) typically read 'Junya', but phonetically assigned here. Combines intense 'purity/honesty' with the rhythmic stylistic flow of 'ya'.
Kiyonari (粋成) pairs 'chic/essence' (Iki/Sui/Kiyo) with 'become/succeed'. Immensely cool and urban, indicating an effortlessly successful and highly cultured individual.
Kiyonari (清就) merges 'pure/clean' with 'settle/take position'. Suggests an incredibly transparent, deeply honest man who naturally achieves high professional status.
Kiyonobu (希与伸) relies on a rare phonetic group ('hope-give-expand'). An intensely positive string of kanji meant to manifest a remarkably giving and growing life.
Kiyonobu (享伸) couples 'enjoy/receive' with 'expand'. Depicts a profoundly liberated individual who widely receives and expands upon life's great pleasures.
Kiyonobu (恭宣) blends 'respect/reverence' with 'proclaim'. Deeply articulate but fundamentally humble, symbolizing an excellent, morally guided speaker or leader.
Kiyoharu (喜代治) uses 'joyful generation' alongside 'heal/govern'. Highly responsible, indicating a calm, deeply restorative personality managing their community's well-being.
Kiyoharu (喜代春) attaches 'spring' to the 'joyful generation' prefix. Unbelievably warm, bright, and deeply reassuring, evoking blooming cherry blossoms and family love.
Kiyoharu (喜代晴) uses 'clear/fair weather'. Suggests a person of extraordinarily sunny disposition, clearing away the sadness of multiple lifetimes.
Kiyohito (幾代士) builds on literal history ('several generations') ending in 'samurai/gentleman'. Profoundly archaic and respectful of ancient warrior lineages.
Kiyohito (巨人) literally reads 'giant'. Phenomenally straightforward and imposing, wishing for the absolute maximum physical and social stature.
Kiyohito (潔一) marries 'undefiled/pure' with 'one' (read as hito). Extremely stoic and clean, indicating unmatched honor and a flawless, uncorrupted worldview.
Kiyohiro (喜代宏) attaches the massive 'macro' kanji to 'joyful generation'. Strongly envisions an individual expanding his family's joy across immense borders.
Kiyohiro (希世弘) combines 'hope', 'world', and 'vast' (Hiro). Cultivates a shockingly massive, international scope, fit for a visionary or worldly diplomat.
Kiyohiro (記世大) uses 'record', 'world', and 'big'. Strongly literary and grand, intending the boy to naturally leave his mark on the great histories of his era.
Kiyofumi (喜代文) links 'joyful generation' to 'literature/art'. Immensely cultured, denoting an aesthetic or deeply studious person enriching their family history.
Kiyofumi (輝吉文) brings exceptionally dense, triple-kanji auspiciousness ('radiant-lucky-art'). Unbelievably packed with glowing wishes for stellar artistic or academic success.
Kiyofumi (潔史) pairs 'pure/undefiled' with 'history'. Depicts a person entirely uncorrupted, leading an impeccably honest and historically significant life.
Kiyomasa (清雅) merges 'pure' with 'elegant/graceful'. Exceptionally handsome, invoking aristocratic refinement and absolute moral clarity.
Kiyomasa (清匡) pairs 'pure' with 'correct/assist' (Masa). Deeply principled, depicting a strictly honest individual who guides others onto righteous paths.
Kiyomasa (清勝) uses 'pure' and 'victory' (made famous by Kato Kiyomasa). Strikingly martial, signaling an invincible, honorable warrior spirit.
Kinichi (勤一) couples 'diligence/serve' with 'one'. A profoundly hard-working, entirely dependable name for a first son tasked with great responsibility.
Kinichi (均一) uses 'even/average' and 'one'. Highlights an impossibly stable, entirely unshakeable individual who judges all things evenly.
Kinichi (錦一) marries 'brocade' (beautifully woven fabric) with 'one'. Exceptionally opulent and artistic, symbolizing a lavishly bright and colorful life.
Kinji (錦司) uses 'brocade' with 'rule/govern'. Blends striking aesthetic beauty with the strong organizational capability of a capable leader.
Kinji (錦寿) pairs 'brocade' with 'longevity' (read 'Ji'). Absolutely overflowing with blessings, aiming for an endlessly brilliant, decorated, and long life.
Kinji (欣伺) utilizes 'rejoice/pleasure' entirely harmonized with 'pay respect/visit' (Ji). Extremely gentle and thoughtful, pointing to a respectful, continuously happy nature.
Kunio (郁男) pairs 'culture/fragrance' (Kuni) with 'man'. Phenomenally refined, pointing towards an artist or scholar possessing deep cultural sophistication.
Kunio (郁夫) swaps the ending for 'husband' (Fu/O). Maintains the exact cultured vibrance, but is perceived as slightly softer and more deeply romantic.
Kunio (一夫) is typically read 'Kazuo', but 'Kunio' is an alternative reading. Supremely grounded, pointing to an unflinching, completely singular leading male.
Kunikazu (久二一) practically plays a game with numbers: 'long-two-one'. Exceptionally weird and mathematically dense, feeling anciently phonetic.
Kunikazu (訓一) merges 'instructions/teachings' with 'one'. Highly principled and strictly disciplined, perfect for a young man meant to guide others morally.
Kunikazu (訓和) combines 'teachings' with 'harmony' (Kazu). Profoundly peaceful, implying an individual who unites communities through gentle, educational leadership.
Kunishige (国重) literally means 'national weight'. Immensely imposing and intensely historical, suggesting a leader carrying the grave responsibility of a region.
Kunishige (邦栄) places 'home country' with 'flourish'. Absolutely bursting with high aspirations, meaning the boy will bring flourishing glory to his homeland.
Kunishige (邦重) translates 'nation/state' alongside 'heavy'. Functionally identical to the heavier '国重', carrying the sheer dignity of high-level statesmanship.
Kunihiko (郁彦) pairs 'culture/fragrance' (Kuni) with the classic 'boy/prince'. Extremely eloquent and artistic, raising the standard phonetic construct to a high noble standard.
